method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
Howland Island supports sparse vegetation adapted to its low, sandy coral terrain, scant rainfall, constant wind, intense sun, and lack of natural freshwater.[1][4] Its recorded flora includes scattered grasses, shrubs, low-growing pisonia trees, and prostrate vines.[1] Low groves of decaying and dead kou trees were noted in 1942, but a visitor in 2000 described the island as a flat coral-sand plain with no trees.[1] The island provides foraging, nesting, and roosting habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and marine wildlife.[1][3] It is surrounded by a narrow active fringing reef, which forms part of the island’s marine habitat and is visible as breakers encircling the shore.[3][4] Howland is part of the United States National Wildlife Refuge system and the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, with public access and extractive activities restricted to protect its wildlife and surrounding marine resources.[3][5]

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent residents and no conventional local economy; public entry is restricted and allowed only by special permit, with use principally by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service.[4] The island is not used for farming, industry, or settlement because it is managed as a strict nature reserve and is part of protected federal conservation areas.[1][3] Its only notable historical extractive activity was guano mining, which was carried out by American and British interests before 1890.[3] Phosphorite and guano are listed as the island’s mineral resources, but the available sources describe no current commercial extraction.[4] The surrounding Howland–Baker exclusive economic zone covers about 425,700 square kilometres, although the island’s present significance is chiefly conservation-based rather than commercial.[4][1] Scientific and management activities focus on monitoring seabirds, marine wildlife, coral reefs, and reef ecosystems, contributing to ecological research and resource protection.[1][3]

==Time zone==
Because Howland Island is uninhabited, it has no local civil time zone, but it is commonly assigned to the nautical time zone UTC−12:00, known as International Date Line West (IDLW).[1][4] In nautical time-zone notation, UTC−12:00 corresponds to the letter Y, or “Yankee”, and lists Baker Island and Howland Island among its primary jurisdictions.[3] Baker Island and Howland Island are the only areas identified with the IDLW time zone.[1][4] As a result, they are among the last places on Earth where any given calendar day ends.[4][5] The time zone is associated with the “Anywhere on Earth” calendar convention, under which a deadline or date remains valid until it has passed everywhere on Earth, effectively ending when it ends on Howland and Baker Islands.[1][5] Baker and Howland Islands are 26 hours behind some territories of Kiribati, which lie on the opposite side of the International Date Line.[2]

==History==
Howland Island may have been visited or inhabited by Polynesians before modern recorded contact, but any such settlement had been abandoned long before the island’s nineteenth-century occupation.[4] American sailor Edmund Fanning is credited in one account with discovering the island in 1791, and the United States claimed or acquired it during the mid-nineteenth-century guano era.[1][4] American guano interests briefly occupied the island for extraction, but no permanent settlement developed.[4]

In the mid-1930s, land was cleared for unpaved landing strips intended to support transpacific air routes, and the airfield was improved for Amelia Earhart’s planned 1937 refueling stop during her round-the-world flight.[4] A small navigation beacon, later associated with Earhart, was also built in 1937.[4][5] On 2 July 1937, Earhart was expected to arrive at Howland, and radio transmissions from her aircraft were heard there, but she never reached the island.[4] Her disappearance made Howland Island closely associated with one of the best-known mysteries in aviation history.[1][4]

During World War II, Japanese aircraft bombed Howland Island in 1941, killing two colonists, and a later submarine attack shelled Itascatown and damaged the light beacon.[5] The remaining colonists were evacuated after the attack, and the island’s station was inactive by 1942.[4][5] Human occupation did not resume after the war, apart from brief visits for beacon restoration and scientific work.[5] In 1974, Howland Island was declared a National Wildlife Refuge under the jurisdiction of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service.[5]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The refuge is administered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, with management centered on conservation of the island’s terrestrial habitats and surrounding reef ecosystems.[1][2] Scientific access is primarily for monitoring and research, including expeditions by the Fish and Wildlife Service, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, and the U.S. Coast Guard to study biological diversity on land and in adjacent waters.[1]  

Federal refuge planning has emphasized biological surveys, habitat assessment, and scientific research rather than recreational visitation or other public uses.[5] Management proposals for Howland, Baker, and Jarvis included more frequent biological surveys, efforts to attract rare seabird species, and additional marine habitat exploration.[5] The refuge’s fringing reef and surrounding waters are also of scientific interest for reef mapping, ecosystem-health assessment, and bathymetric study using high-resolution imagery.[2]

==Earhart Light==
The structure is a small navigation beacon, often described as a lighthouse, that was built in 1937 on Howland Island.[3][4] Although associated with the island’s aviation preparations, it did not have a long operational life and has been inactive since 1942; the remaining structure is now regarded as a day beacon rather than an active lighthouse.[3] The beacon was damaged during World War II, when Japanese attacks struck the island and rendered it unusable.[2][4] It was later rebuilt or restored as a memorial, and restoration visits also added the names of the two colonists killed during the wartime attack.[2][4]

==Image gallery==
A map in the gallery can place Howland Island in relation to nearby Baker Island, which lies 66 km away in the central Pacific region southwest of Hawaiʻi.[5]  

Astronaut photography from ISS Expedition 10 provides an oblique view of Howland Island taken on 3 December 2004 from an altitude of about 192 nautical miles, using an 800 mm lens.[2][3]  

NASA’s caption for the ISS image notes that the island is composed of coral fragments and is surrounded by an active fringing reef, visible as white breakers around the shoreline.[3]  

Such high-resolution astronaut images of reefs are used for reef and island mapping, ecosystem assessment, and bathymetric studies.[3]  

Images of Earhart Light are also relevant to the gallery because the United States Lighthouse Society identifies the structure as a notable landmark associated with Howland Island’s role in Amelia Earhart’s planned flight.[4]
